From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Arrive Alive Cup](/w/index.php?title=Arrive_Alive_Cup&redirect=no "Arrive Alive Cup"))

Australian youth rugby league competition

NRL Schoolboy Cup

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Sport | [Rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") |
| Instituted | 1975 |
| Country | Australia ([Australian Rugby League Commission](/wiki/Australian_Rugby_League_Commission "Australian Rugby League Commission")) |
| Holders | [Keebra Park State High School](/wiki/Keebra_Park_State_High_School "Keebra Park State High School")  (2025) |
| Most titles | [St Gregory's College, Campbelltown](/wiki/St_Gregory%27s_College,_Campbelltown "St Gregory's College, Campbelltown") (9 titles) |
| Website | [GIO Schoolboy Cup website](http://www.playnrl.com/schools/schoolboy-competitions/gio-schooolboy-cup-match-previews/) |
| Broadcast partner | [Fox Sports](/wiki/Fox_Sports_Australia "Fox Sports Australia") |

The **NRL Schoolboy Cup** is the premier [secondary schools](/wiki/Education_in_Australia#Secondary_schools "Education in Australia") [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") competition in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), held annually since 1975. Formerly known under such names as the **Amco Shield**, **Commonwealth Bank Cup**, **Arrive Alive Cup** and **GIO Schoolboy Cup** nearly 450 schools from throughout Australia compete in the knock-out competition.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The player of the tournament is awarded the **Peter Sterling Medal**, renamed in 1996 after [Peter Sterling](/wiki/Peter_Sterling_(rugby_league) "Peter Sterling (rugby league)"), who won the medal in 1978, for [Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield](/wiki/Patrician_Brothers%27_College,_Fairfield "Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield"). The competition is televised on [Fox Sports](/wiki/Fox_Sports_Australia "Fox Sports Australia") and [The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph").

The most successful school is [St Gregory's College, Campbelltown](/wiki/St_Gregory%27s_College,_Campbelltown "St Gregory's College, Campbelltown"), with nine titles, followed by [Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield](/wiki/Patrician_Brothers%27_College,_Fairfield "Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield") with six. The most recent champions are [Palm Beach Currumbin State High School](/wiki/Palm_Beach_Currumbin_State_High_School "Palm Beach Currumbin State High School"), who won their fourth title in 2024.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NRL_Schoolboy_Cup&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The competition was founded in 1975 as the Amco Shield. The inaugural winners were [Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield](/wiki/Patrician_Brothers%27_College,_Fairfield "Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield") who defeated [Blacktown High School](/wiki/Blacktown_Boys_High_School "Blacktown Boys High School") in the final, 16–8. The match was played at [Leichhardt Oval](/wiki/Leichhardt_Oval "Leichhardt Oval") as a curtain raiser to the 1975 [Amco Cup](/wiki/Amco_Cup "Amco Cup") final between [Eastern Suburbs](/wiki/Eastern_Suburbs_Roosters "Eastern Suburbs Roosters") and [Parramatta](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Originally, the televised matches of the competition were played and recorded as the curtain raisers to mid week [Amco Cup](/wiki/Amco_Cup "Amco Cup") games. After the midweek competitions died out, the televised matches were played prior to [Friday Night Football](/wiki/Friday_Night_Football_(NRL) "Friday Night Football (NRL)") and were shown on [Channel 9](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network") in the weeks leading up to the [NRL Grand Final](/wiki/NRL_Grand_Final "NRL Grand Final"). The games are currently played and recorded midweek and shown on [Fox Sports](/wiki/Fox_Sports_Australia "Fox Sports Australia"), with commentary by [Andrew Voss](/wiki/Andrew_Voss "Andrew Voss").

In 1978, [Padua College, Brisbane](/wiki/Padua_College,_Brisbane "Padua College, Brisbane") were the first [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") school and first school from outside of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") to make the final. They lost 19–3 to [Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield](/wiki/Patrician_Brothers%27_College,_Fairfield "Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield").

In 1980, [St Gregory's College, Campbelltown](/wiki/St_Gregory%27s_College,_Campbelltown "St Gregory's College, Campbelltown") became the first school to win back-to-back titles. They later became the first school to win three titles in a row from 1989 to 1991.

In 1982 [Holy Cross College, Ryde](/wiki/Holy_Cross_College,_Ryde "Holy Cross College, Ryde") became the first school to play in four consecutive finals from 1979. They lost three of their four finals, with their only victory coming against [Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield](/wiki/Patrician_Brothers%27_College,_Fairfield "Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield") in 1981.

In 1996, [Erindale College, Canberra](/wiki/Wanniassa#Erindale_College "Wanniassa") became the first school from the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory") to reach the final. A year later they became the first school from outside of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") to win the competition. They won the competition again in 1998, beating [Parramatta Marist High School](/wiki/Parramatta_Marist_High_School "Parramatta Marist High School") in both years.

In 2001, [Palm Beach Currumbin State High School](/wiki/Palm_Beach_Currumbin_State_High_School "Palm Beach Currumbin State High School") became the first [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") school to win the competition. Brisbane's [Wavell State High School](/wiki/Wavell_State_High_School "Wavell State High School") became the second a year later in 2002.

[Endeavour Sports High School](/wiki/Endeavour_Sports_High_School "Endeavour Sports High School") won the competition three years in a row from 2004 to 2006, becoming the first school to do so since 1991.

In 2015, [Townsville's](/wiki/Townsville,_Queensland "Townsville, Queensland") [Kirwan State High School](/wiki/Kirwan_State_High_School "Kirwan State High School") became the first school from [North Queensland](/wiki/North_Queensland "North Queensland") to win the competition, defeating [Patrician Brothers' College, Blacktown](/wiki/Patrician_Brothers%27_College,_Blacktown "Patrician Brothers' College, Blacktown"), who were in their fourth straight final, losing the last three in a row.

In 2016, [Westfields Sports High School](/wiki/Westfields_Sports_High_School "Westfields Sports High School") won the competition for the first time.

In 2017, [Keebra Park State High School](/wiki/Keebra_Park_State_High_School "Keebra Park State High School") became first Queensland school to win the competition three times, becoming the most successful school in the state. A year later, Palm Beach Currumbin won their third title, drawing level with Keebra Park. In 2019, [Kirwan State High School](/wiki/Kirwan_State_High_School "Kirwan State High School") won the competition for the second time, making it three straight years a Queensland school has won.

In 2020 and 2021, due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), no national finals were held for the first time in the competition's history.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In 2022, after a three year absence, the national final returned, with [Ipswich State High School](/wiki/Ipswich_State_High_School "Ipswich State High School") defeating [Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield](/wiki/Patrician_Brothers%27_College,_Fairfield "Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield") to win their first title.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In 2025, [Keebra Park State High School](/wiki/Keebra_Park_State_High_School "Keebra Park State High School") defeated [Patrician Brothers' College, Blacktown](/wiki/Patrician_Brothers%27_College,_Blacktown "Patrician Brothers' College, Blacktown") extending the Blacktown side’s run of heartbreak with an eighth finals defeat and only one victory to show for it.

## Naming rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NRL_Schoolboy_Cup&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Naming rights")]

Due to sponsorship, the Schoolboy Cup has gone under many different names since first being held in 1975. Originally known as the **Amco Shield**, it has been known as the **NRL Schoolboy Cup** since 2018.

* Amco Shield (1975–1979)
* [Commonwealth Bank](/wiki/Commonwealth_Bank "Commonwealth Bank") Cup (1980–1996)
* [Aussie Home Loans](/wiki/Aussie_Home_Loans "Aussie Home Loans") Cup (1997–1999)
* [Nutri-Grain](/wiki/Nutri-Grain "Nutri-Grain") Cup (2000–2002)
* Arrive Alive Cup (2003–2009)
* National Schoolboy Cup (2010–2011)
* [GIO](/wiki/GIO_Insurance "GIO Insurance") Schoolboy Cup (2012–2017)[[5]](#cite_note-5)
* NRL Schoolboy Cup (2018–present)

## Format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NRL_Schoolboy_Cup&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Format")]

The competition is divided into two conferences. The Northern Conference, comprising schools from [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), is further divided into smaller competitions: the Aaron Payne Cup, the Allan Langer Trophy, and the Dolphin Cup. The winners of these cups compete against each other, with the victor being crowned the Queensland Schoolboy Cup Champion. The Southern Conference, known as the [Peter Mulholland](/wiki/Peter_Mulholland "Peter Mulholland") Cup, includes schools from [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory") and [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Australia "Victoria, Australia"). The winners of the Queensland Schoolboy Trophy and the Peter Mulholland Cup then face off in the National Schoolboy Final, with the winner being crowned the National Schoolboy Champion.

## Peter Sterling Medal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NRL_Schoolboy_Cup&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Peter Sterling Medal")]

The Peter Sterling Medal is awarded to the most outstanding player in the competition. Sterling, one of rugby league's most decorated players, won the award in 1978 while attending [Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield](/wiki/Patrician_Brothers%27_College,_Fairfield "Patrician Brothers' College, Fairfield"). In 1996, the medal was renamed in his honour.
